result:
The moon was a silver coin wedged in the sky’s dark pocket the night I learned that silence has flavors.  
I was eight, sitting between my grandmother’s knees in the backyard of a house that has since been sold, renamed, repainted into someone else’s memory. A warm wind licked the pecan leaves above us; July was busy ripening their nuts. My grandmother’s fingers were small mallets in my tight plaits, each tug a soft punctuation mark in an otherwise wordless dusk.  

She had just told me dogs remember every scent because smells ride light-waves to the stars and back again while we sleep, and I sediments of aroma wait for wet noses to decode them like books of braille. Her Creole floated inside the story like anise and cane-smoke. The porch lamp buzzed, a miniature galaxy occupied by one shrill cicada, and quiet fell in sheets. That silence tasted—an incongruous word my mouth formed without speaking—like cornmeal dusted across fingers and the memory of Christmas chocolate left in a coat pocket until spring. I swallowed it quietly because no eight-year-old wants to admit the impossible is obvious.  

Later, when language thickened its gravy and seasoned my tongue with politics and CVs, I kept returning to that flavor, asking others, “What does silence taste like to you?” Most laughed politely, the corners of their mouths twitching like badly mended curtains. A librarian paused mid-re-shelving and answered, “Cardboard and apology.” A tattoo artist, wiping blood from the skin between a shoulder blade and a dream said, “Copper and ozone, right before a rain you know won’t come.” A veteran I interviewed flattened the word into “charcoal, because nothing burns quite as long as a question you never asked the ones who didn’t make it home.”  

Silence collects us the way velvet collects lint—first slowly, then overwhelmingly—until you cannot lift your own name out of it without feeling its weight. And yet it travels. I carry it across airports now, tucked between passport stamps like pressed violets. The hum of fluorescent lights in Terminal B tastes like refrigerated lengths of tin, whereas the roar-off of runway sixteen at LAX is carbonated water sprinkled with jet-fuel and regret. My recorder, once dependent on cassette ribbons the color of rusted dawn, fits inside a phone and saves spherical waves of absence: a child’s unanswered bedtime question in Aleppo, a cormorant’s wings collapsing behind oil sheen off the coast of Louisiana, the click of keys my mother no longer presses. By syncing time stamps and sipping headphones, I curate an atlas of all the silences ever bequeathed to me.  

I used to think recording was preserving; now I suspect it is translation. Sound engineers call the leftover room tone “noise floor,” the hiss of present absence. Editing software renders it a straight neon line, the heart’s flatline wearing earbuds. Someday someone will play that emptiness and mistake it for forgetting.  

There is a museum in Reykjavík dedicated entirely to unopened letters. A dim hallway of glass cases glows like a constellation of unread intentions. I pinned a handwritten label beneath each display and offered curators my library of flavors, asking that visitors taste the silence with their eyelids, the way my grandmother tasted wind while combing secrets into my scalp. The director blushed and promised to consider it, but the next week I found my suggestions taped inside a drawer marked “miscellaneous souls.” On the flight back, clouds braided themselves into the faux-fur of sky and I practiced chewing nothing, my mouth full of padlocks.  

Agoraphobia isn’t fear of open spaces, a therapist tells me. It’s dread of the space within becoming wider than the horizon. She writes prescriptions for serotonin, but I pocket the paper and instead pay a violinist on Union Square to play “Ashokan Farewell” forty-two times without pause. Between bow strokes, unstruck strings shimmer with acoustical shadows, the negative of music. I lean my forehead against the subway gate and let its iron grid imprint maps on my skin. Every resonance tastes like tinfoil cut with lemon zest—bright armor over a tang that erodes. Passersby drop quarters. They think grief belongs to someone else.  

When the pandemic folds the city like origami, silence acquires mass. Elevators become confessionals minus priests. News anchors fumble at unwrapped moments, holding microphones against open mouths that produce no crowd. Sirens thread through masked avenues like red embroidery on slate. Somewhere a child counts seventeen ambulances in an hour and wonders if optimists still measure distance in hope. My apartment becomes a terrarium for solitude; I water it with memory and watch it grow translucent. At 3:12 a.m., I hear refrigerator coils humming—my appliances, lonely, singing to themselves in mechanical falsetto.  

In dreams, my grandmother offers me a silver coin. “Flip it,” she whispers. Heads, the past resumes its everyday heartbeats; tails, the unsaid outspeaks the said, and history trembles into a new accent. The coin rotates in slow motion, silvering my palms. Mid-flip, I taste that childhood flavor again—cornmeal and lost chocolate—and realize silence has been circulating the whole time like blood, feeding the dreams of words asleep inside dictionaries. When the coin lands, I wake before it lands.  

I begin mailing empty envelopes to strangers whose addresses I find by paging through old high school yearbooks with my eyes closed. Each envelope is sealed with wax the color of wet asphalt. No letter inside, just the carbon-black of someone else’s exhalation. On the back flap I draw a tiny moon wearing one sneaker. The postal worker shrugs; the weight registers “one forever stamp.” I imagine recipients holding the envelope to their ear, expecting the rustle of confession or apology, then frowning at the cotton hush. Maybe one of them will taste cardboard and apology. Maybe another will smell copper before a rain, remembered sudden and soft, the way a dog revives love from under a porch.  

Yesterday the sky over Brooklyn lowered its lid until everything tasted boiled, metallic, like dimes dissolving on tongues. I watched a wedding through binoculars balanced on the fire escape: intoxicated guests forming a distanced semicircle, bride in moonlight-colored mask, groom repeating vows into a microphone that delayed syllables by half seconds; the automatic delay sounded like hesitation re-echoed until it emerged from their mouths as a question. Afterward they released fifteen biodegradable balloons that faltered upward as though embarrassed by gravity. The silence left behind tasted exactly like aluminum and second chances. I wished I could spoon-feed it to the librarian whose cardboard melancholy must by now be damp with tropical storms of read books.  

There is physics in this: sound compresses molecules into each other, but silence pulls them apart—negative pressure, a brief starvation of matter. Living cells know the difference; leave them hungry long enough and they cannibalize their own mitochondria. Maybe hearts do the same. Maybe that’s why we fill the emptiness with podcasts, with fluorescent hum, with violin loops—anything to postpone autophagy of spirit.  

Once I tried to weigh one final silence, the kind reserved for deathbeds and demolished opera halls. I borrowed a scale used for diamonds, calibrated to microcarets. I emptied my lungs into a mason jar at midnight, sealed it, placed the jar on the tray. The digital readout blinked “0.0000.” Not nothing, but too small for repayment. I labeled the jar with adhesive tape and wrote “grandmother” in ballpoint even though her recipes never needed metrics. The jar now sits beside a dying spider plant on my desk. Occasionally I unscrew the lid, dip in a fingernail, and nibble what drips out: hot-sweet beignet, cane-smoke, and that secret salt burned off every story before it finds the tongue.  

One thousand words ago I promised to spend this last paragraph landing somewhere definite. Yet I am still mid-flip, the coin suspended in moonlight or memory or possibly the white space between these very lines—an extra second you could slip a whole life into if you breathe just right. Taste it. The flavor is specific unknowing, a breadcrumb shaped like tomorrow leading nowhere but to more paths flavored by silences we are still too busy naming. The pecan tree is gone; so is the house. But inside the pocket of the sky the silver coin remains, its obverse side tasting of yes, reverse tasting of yes again, both faces singing the hum that links one word to the next when both end in exhaustion, and so the night arrives where even the coin falls quiet.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
